figures.caption: Table 1: Overview of fine-tuning strategies for pretrained models.

Figure 1: Schematic of the AB-UPT architecture reproduced from the original paper [15]. Geometry supernodes, surface anchors, and volume anchors form three branches connected via self- and cross-attention. Note: this figure shows the original architecture for conceptual reference only; the model used in this study (Table 3) is deeper and wider, having been re-calibrated for production vehicle geometries.
Figure 2: Sample geometric variations of the Mid-size SUV family. Ten geometric parameters are varied at 10 levels each, yielding ∼100 configurations per family.
Figure 3: Normalized frontal area vs. Cd distribution for five vehicle families. Dashed ellipses indicate approximate 2σ contours.
Table 5: Experimental matrix and strategy comparison. Fine-tuning rows are repeated for all five leave-one-out configurations (15 runs total).
Figure 4: In-distribution performance of the pretrained model on the four training families. Left: pressure-drag R2 on held-out test splits. Right: MAE and RMSE of predicted drag force.

Figure 5: Surface pressure predictions of the pretrained model (leave-Large-SUV-#3-out checkpoint; both vehicles are in-distribution) on baseline configurations. Top: Small Crossover (RMSE = 19.5 Pa, drag error = 0.43N). Bottom: Large SUV #1 (RMSE = 21.5 Pa, drag error = 3.9N). Color range: [−300, 300] Pa (pressure), [0, 10] Pa (error). Errors concentrate near geometric discontinuities, confirming the model captures smooth pressure topology with high fidelity.

Figure 6: Zero-shot inference on the held-out Large SUV #3 family using the pretrained model without any finetuning. Despite a weak positive correlation indicating partial physics understanding, predictions exhibit strong bias and large scatter (R2 = −5.27, MAE = 75.9N).
Table 7: FFT test-set results across five leave-one-out experiments. Force R2 is computed from integrated surface forces; field metrics are pointwise surface errors.

Table 8: FFT learning rate sensitivity: test-set metrics for two representative vehicles at three learning rates. All metrics degrade monotonically with lower learning rates, confirming that FFT’s failure is not a hyperparameter tuning issue.

Figure 7: FFT pressure drag: predicted vs. ground-truth for five held-out families. Filled circles: test set; open circles: training set. Performance varies from R2 = 0.70 (Large SUV #1) to −0.17 (Small Crossover).

Figure 8: LFT pressure drag: predicted vs. ground-truth for five held-out families. Four of five families yield R2 < 0. Training points (open circles) also fail, confirming fundamental inability to learn.
Table 9: LFT test-set results across five leave-one-out experiments. Force R2 is computed from integrated surface forces; field metrics are pointwise surface errors.
Table 10: LoRA rank sweep on the Large SUV #3 target family. Performance plateaus at r = 64.
Table 11: 5-fold cross-validation results for LoRA (r = 64) on Large SUV #3.

Figure 9: LoRA pressure drag: predicted vs. ground-truth for five held-out families. All families achieve R2 > 0.83 with tight clustering around the identity line, demonstrating consistent generalization.
Table 12: LoRA (r = 64) test-set results across five leave-one-out experiments. Force R2 is computed from integrated surface forces; field metrics are pointwise surface errors.

Figure 10: Surface pressure predictions of the LoRA-adapted model (r = 64) on the same baseline configurations as Figure 5. Unlike the pretrained model, here each vehicle is out-of-distribution: the base checkpoint was trained without the target family and adapted with only 20 samples. Top: Small Crossover (RMSE = 27.8 Pa, drag error = 7.5N). Bottom: Large SUV #1 (RMSE = 26.8Pa, drag error = 18.7N). Color range: [−300, 300] Pa (pressure), [0, 10] Pa (error). Despite the harder setting, LoRA achieves comparable visual fidelity; its aggregate advantage manifests across the full DOE test distribution.
Figure 11: Per-vehicle test-set R2 comparison. Left: pressure drag; Right: shear drag. LoRA (blue) dominates across all families with minimal variance.
Figure 12: From-scratch training baselines for Large SUV #3 total drag force. Left: 103 samples (train/val R2 = 0.90/0.81, MAE = 10.0 N). Right: 30 samples (test R2 = 0.77, MAE = 12.3 N).
